what_this_pass_proved: The core add-to-target loop is comprehensible without an instruction overlay — players understand 'tap to add, match the number' from first visible state.
Overshoot bounce-back teaches self-correction without creating anxiety — the brief 'Too many!' overlay followed by item reversal lands as a gentle correction, not a punishment.
A 9-second belt duration at Level 1 gives sufficient time for a new player to understand the loop before motion becomes meaningful pressure.
The patience bar is a legible teaching signal for time constraint — players respond to the color shift without being told what it means.
The difficulty ramp across all three parameters (beltDuration, patience, targetPoolMax) is smooth after the L1→L2 spike was fixed. Auditor verdict: smooth_ramp, severity clean.
open_questions: The conveyor generates 12 random items — there is a ~7% probability of no 1-value items (croissants) appearing, which could make low-gap targets unreachable. Does this affect teaching clarity in practice?
Does the 'Next: 🎯X 🎯Y' queue preview in the HUD help players plan ahead, or does it split attention away from the current order?
Is the pastry box the right visual container for the running total, or should the total be displayed more prominently separate from the items?
At Levels 4–5, does the shorter belt duration (3s, 2s) feel punishing to players who were succeeding at Levels 1–2, or does the ramp feel earned?

bugs_fixed.description: beltDuration at Level 1 was 5s — below the 7s teaching minimum. Raised to 9s. Players could not reliably read values off the belt before they scrolled past.
beltDuration L1→L2 spike was 56% (9→4s, exceeding 35% max). targetPoolMax L1→L2 spike was 50% (8→12, exceeding 40% max). Both cascaded into a full ramp fix. Post-fix: all steps smooth.
Title and game header displayed '(Component Architecture)' — a development note visible to players.
Customer speech said 'I'd like X points worth, please!' — 'points' is the wrong vocabulary for a bakery. Changed to 'pastries'.
Duplicate patience bar appeared in the HUD and on the OrderTicket simultaneously. Removed from HUD; kept on OrderTicket where it is contextually adjacent to the customer.
When patience expired and lives remained, the customer left silently — the UI reset with no feedback. Added a 900ms 'Too slow! The customer left.' overlay before advancing.

known_limits: No solvability guarantee on the conveyor belt — items are randomly generated. A belt with no small-value items could make certain targets unreachable in theory.
The customer queue wraps — five customers cycle repeatedly. Long sessions surface the same orders.
Score-based level advancement means slower players may complete a full session without reaching Levels 4–5.
All five pastry types are always available on the belt regardless of level. Introducing value complexity as a difficulty axis (fewer types at Level 1) is deferred to Pass 2.
No hint or help mechanism for stuck players.

auditor_results.notes: Auditor run post-fix against LEVEL_CONFIGS [9,6,4,3,2] / [20,18,15,12,10] / targetPoolMax [8,10,12,15,18]. All steps within thresholds. Spec-driven path confirmed identical to convenience-path result.

next_pass_first_obligation: Prove that players can successfully fill orders for targets ≥ 12 without the belt feeling uncontrollably fast — or determine whether target complexity (not belt speed) should become the primary difficulty axis at Levels 3–5.
